title: Umbilical hernia - NHS
text:
in babies and young children. They can happen when the muscles around the umbilical cord (where the belly button is) do not close properly after birth.
Usually, the hernia will go away as the child grows older.
In adults, an umbilical hernia can be caused by:
living with obesity having multiple pregnancies having a condition that can cause a build-up of fluid inside your tummy (ascites), such as liver disease 
Treatments for an umbilical hernia 
Children do not usually need treatment for an umbilical hernia as the hernia will usually disappear by the time they're 5 years old.
Treatment is usually only needed if the hernia is very big or does not disappear.
Surgery is usually recommended for adults as the hernia is unlikely to get better by itself. But if you're unwell due to a condition like liver disease, then you may not be fit enough to have surgery.
Umbilical hernia repair surgery An umbilical hernia can be treated using a type of surgery called an umbilical hernia repair.
This type of surgery is usually done using general anaesthetic , which means you're asleep while it’s carried out.
During umbilical hernia repair surgery:
Cuts are made in the skin near the hernia The hernia is gently pushed back into place. The gap in the muscles is closed by stitching the muscles together. For larger hernias, a surgical mesh may also be attached to the muscles to help hold them in plac
